metadata.title: Choosing the Right Source of Credit
metadata.subtitle: Compare Lenders, Avoid Predatory Traps, and Save Hundreds on Borrowing Costs

content.title: Choosing the Right Source of Credit
content.subtitle: Compare Lenders, Avoid Predatory Traps, and Save Hundreds on Borrowing Costs

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Marcus needs $1,500 for an emergency car repair to keep his job. With a credit score of 650, he's comparing three options: • Credit union personal loan: 8% APR
• Credit card: 18% APR
• Payday loan advertising 'quick cash': 400% APR (fine print) How can Marcus determine which credit source will cost him the least and keep him out of a debt trap? How can Marcus determine which credit source will cost him the least and keep him out of a debt trap?

How can Marcus determine which credit source will cost him the least and keep him out of a debt trap?

slides.notes:
DISCUSSION PROMPTS:
THE CHALLENGE (5 min):
• Read Marcus's emergency car repair scenario aloud.
• Discussion: "Why might someone choose a payday loan even knowing it costs more?"
• Introduce the concept of predatory lending as exploiting urgent needs.

objectives.description: Compare different credit sources including banks, credit unions, and alternative lenders.
Evaluate credit options based on APR, fees, terms, and total cost of borrowing.
Identify warning signs of predatory lending practices and debt traps.
Calculate the true cost of borrowing from different sources to make informed decisions.

terms.definition: A member-owned, not-for-profit financial cooperative that often offers lower rates than banks because profits go back to members.
terms.example: A credit union might offer 8% APR on a personal loan vs. 12% at a traditional bank.

terms.definition: A lender offering small, short-term loans with extremely high interest rates—often 300-500% APR when annualized.
terms.example: Borrowing $500 for 2 weeks might cost $75 in fees—that's 391% APR!

terms.definition: Unfair lending practices that take advantage of borrowers through deceptive terms, excessive fees, or targeting vulnerable people.
terms.example: Hidden fees, balloon payments, steering to higher rates than you qualify for, pressure to borrow more.

terms.definition: A measure of how likely a borrower is to repay debt, based on credit history, score, income, and existing debt.
terms.example: Higher creditworthiness = lower interest rates and more lending options available to you.

content.headerTitle: Not All Credit Sources Are Equal

leftPanel.title: The Credit Source Hierarchy
paragraphs: From lowest to highest cost (generally):
1. Credit Unions: Member-owned, lowest rates
2. Banks: Traditional, competitive rates
3. Online Lenders: Convenient, rates vary widely
4. Credit Cards: Easy access, 15-25% APR
5. Consumer Finance: 20-35% APR, fewer requirements
6. Payday/Title Lenders: 300-500%+ APR—AVOID!

highlightBox.text: The easier it is to get approved, the more expensive the credit usually is.

leftPanel.title: How the Trap Works
paragraphs: Payday loans seem simple: borrow $500, pay back $575 in 2 weeks. That's only $75!
The problem: Most people can't pay it back in 2 weeks AND cover their other bills.
So they 'roll over': Pay $75 fee, extend another 2 weeks. Repeat.
After 4 rollovers (8 weeks): Paid $300 in fees, still owe $500!
The average payday borrower is in debt for 5 months of the year.

highlightBox.text: That $75 'fee' on $500 for 2 weeks = 391% APR. It's not a small fee—it's predatory interest.

stats.label: Payday loans rolled over or reborrowed

layoutData.title: Same Amount, VERY Different Costs
paragraphs: Let's calculate what Marcus actually pays for his $1,500 car repair:
bulletPoints: Credit Union (8% APR, 12 months): Monthly payment $130, Total interest $60 → Total: $1,560
Credit Card (18% APR, 12 months): Monthly payment $138, Total interest $156 → Total: $1,656
Payday Loan (400% APR, 2 weeks): Single term $1,730... but if rolled over 4 times → Total: $2,420

keyPoint.text: 💰 The payday loan costs Marcus $860 MORE than the credit union—enough to cover another repair!

layoutData.title: Red Flags to Watch For
paragraphs: Protect yourself by recognizing these warning signs:
bulletPoints: No credit check required: Legitimate lenders assess your ability to repay
Pressure to decide NOW: Good lenders give you time to review terms
Focus on payment, not total cost: 'Only $50/week!' hides the true expense
Excessive fees buried in fine print: Origination fees, processing fees, early payoff penalties
Asset-based lending: Car title loans can leave you without transportation
Encouraging you to borrow more: 'You qualify for $5,000!' when you asked for $500

keyPoint.text: 🚨 If it seems too easy or too good to be true, it probably is. Always read the fine print!

leftPanel.title: Better Options Exist
paragraphs: Before turning to payday lenders, consider:
Negotiate with the creditor: Many will set up payment plans or delay due dates
Credit union payday alternative loans: PALs offer $200-$1,000 at reasonable rates
Payment plan with provider: Medical offices often offer 0% payment plans
Borrow from retirement (last resort): 401k loans are to yourself
Side gig income: Even a few hours can cover immediate needs

infoCard.title: Ask These Questions First:
items: Can I negotiate a payment plan?
Have I checked credit union rates?
What's the TOTAL cost, not just monthly?
Can I build emergency fund after this?

slides.notes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS:

1. "Payday loans are only for irresponsible people."
   → Many borrowers are working people facing emergencies without access to traditional credit. The problem is the product design, not the borrower.

2. "Credit unions are only for certain jobs or groups."
   → Many credit unions now have open membership or easy-to-meet criteria. Check local options—most people can join at least one.

3. "Banks always have the best rates."
   → Credit unions often beat bank rates because they're not-for-profit. Online lenders can also be competitive for borrowers with good credit.

layoutData.title: How to Compare Credit Options
paragraphs: When you need to borrow, follow these steps:
bulletPoints: Check your credit score first: Know what you qualify for before shopping
Get quotes from multiple sources: Credit union, bank, online lender (rate shopping within 14-45 days counts as one inquiry)
Compare APR, not just interest rate: APR includes fees in the true cost
Calculate total cost: Multiply monthly payment × months, add all fees
Read the fine print: Prepayment penalties? Variable rates? Late fees?
Consider the term: Longer terms = lower payments but MORE total interest

keyPoint.text: 📊 A 10% APR loan for 5 years costs MORE in total interest than 12% APR for 3 years. Do the math!

scenario.name: Marcus's Smart Choice
paragraphs: Marcus has a 650 credit score—not great, but fair. He needs $1,500 for his car repair urgently to keep his job.
The payday lender offers same-day approval. The credit union needs 3 days but offers 8% APR. Marcus talks to his employer and gets a 3-day extension on his deadline.

scenario.name: Jasmine's Home Purchase
paragraphs: Jasmine has excellent credit (780) and is buying her first home. She gets quotes from her bank (6.5% APR), credit union (6.25% APR), and an online lender (6.75% APR).
On a $300,000 30-year mortgage, the difference between 6.25% and 6.75% is significant over time.

content.question:
For each scenario, which credit source would you recommend and why?

1. Derek has fair credit (620) and a $2,000 medical bill. Payday lender offers same-day; credit union needs 3 days.

2. Aisha wants $15,000 to start a food truck business but banks rejected her.

3. You need $500 for textbooks and have no credit history.

Imagine a friend is about to take out a payday loan because they need money fast.

What would you say to them?

What alternatives would you suggest they explore first?

questions.question: Which type of lender typically offers the lowest interest rates to qualified borrowers, and why?
Explain why credit unions often offer better rates than commercial banks.
A payday loan for $500 charges $75 in fees for 2 weeks. If rolled over 4 times, what's the total cost?
List three warning signs that a lender might be predatory.

slides.notes:
ANSWER KEY:

1. C (Credit unions)

2. Credit unions are not-for-profit and member-owned. Profits go back to members as lower rates and fewer fees rather than to shareholders.

3. $161 × 36 = $5,796 + $100 fee = $5,896 total cost

4. Credit union total: $1,560. Payday 4× rollover: $2,420. Savings: $860

5. Response should include specific alternatives (credit union, payment plan, community resources) and explain WHY payday loans are dangerous (debt cycle, extreme costs).

takeaways.title: Credit Unions Often Win
takeaways.description: Member-owned, not-for-profit structure means lower rates. Check credit union options before banks.

takeaways.title: Avoid Payday Lenders
takeaways.description: 300-500% APR creates debt traps. The convenience isn't worth the cost—explore alternatives first.

takeaways.title: Compare Total Cost
takeaways.description: Don't just look at monthly payments. Calculate total repayment including all fees and interest.

takeaways.title: Recognize Predatory Signs
takeaways.description: No credit check, pressure tactics, hidden fees, and 'too easy' approval are red flags.

main.title: Credit Source Comparison Challenge
main.description: Marcus made a smart choice—but can you help others do the same? Tomorrow you'll analyze real borrowing scenarios, calculate true costs, and develop strategies to avoid predatory lending traps.

steps: Match borrowing scenarios to the most appropriate credit source
Calculate and compare total costs from different lenders
Identify predatory lending warning signs in loan offers
Analyze payday loan rollover costs vs. alternatives
Create a personal credit source ranking with explanations
